design_philosophy.core_loop: Navigate levels destroying horror-themed enemies, rescue neighbors before they're killed, collect weapons and power-ups to combat varied monster types
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Race against enemy threats to save neighbors; time pressure from daytime-to-nightfall transitions that permanently alter rescue conditions
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Teenage heroes armed with unconventional weaponry fighting classic horror movie monsters in suburban and exotic locations
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Weapon-enemy matchmaking system tied to horror film lore (silverware effective on werewolves, crucifixes on vampires); mandatory neighbor rescue mechanic creates protection responsibility; two-player cooperative focus with shared objective

difficulty.lives_system: Lives awarded through point accumulation; game lost when all lives exhausted or all neighbors killed
difficulty.checkpointing: Per-level progression; at least one neighbor must be saved per level to advance
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Scoring system controls resource acquisition (lives and extra neighbors); players choose which neighbors to prioritize and which weapons to collect

progression.upgrade_system: Temporary power-ups collected in-level: speed enhancement potions, monster transformation potions; permanent weapon unlocks through level progression
progression.unlock_mechanism: 48 sequential stages with seven optional bonus levels; weapon variety increases across levels
replayability_hooks: Multiple neighbor types with varying point values create scoring optimization challenges
Two-player cooperative mode offers different strategy than single-player
Daytime-to-nightfall transitions alter level conditions (tourists become unsaveable werewolves)
Optional bonus levels provide alternate content
Different weapon availability between SNES and Genesis versions

world_building_techniques: Level theming through location variety: suburbs, shopping malls, pyramids, haunted castles
Enemy design rooted in 1950s classic horror and modern slasher films (Friday the 13th, Texas Chain Saw Massacre references)
Weapon design reflects horror film tropes (crucifixes, flamethrowers, silverware as monster countermeasures)
Neighbor type diversity creates narrative texture (barbecue chefs, teachers, babies, tourists, archeologists, soldiers, dogs, cheerleaders)

boss_design.philosophy: Final confrontation with Dr. Tongue, the mad scientist antagonist who created the monster outbreak; boss represents culmination of suburban horror escalation

faction_indicators: Zeke and Julie as teenage hero archetypes standing against establishment horror
Neighbor types (tourists, soldiers, teachers) represent community classes needing protection
danger_signals: Dr. Tongue as threatening authority figure unleashing chaos
Chainsaw-wielding maniacs and pod people (alien clones) represent bodily autonomy threats
Nightfall transformation mechanic: external force removes character agency (tourists become unsaveable werewolves)
environmental_storytelling: Progression through suburban domesticity corrupted by horror incursion
Location escalation from neighborhoods to malls to exotic locales reflects growing scope of threat
Weapon variety available in each level telegraphs enemy threat profile
Neighbor rescue mechanic makes player responsible for community protection
